sentences.text: Yes.
Might as well, even at that [INAUDIBLE], 15,000 people, 2,000 of us arrived at the border of Germany.
The rest of us was killed one way or another.
Like I said, you know, [INAUDIBLE] was a worse nightmare than anybody can have.
But I can imagine, you know, what happened during the walk.
One day, I was again trying to run away [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to go back to [INAUDIBLE] where, you know, there was the killing.
It was hard to tell [INAUDIBLE] how I saved my life and two other friend of mine's life at that time.
Like I said, I was in the chimney.
And I find out what's happening.
I came down and I'm telling my two other friends, listen.
We have no choice.
We've got to do something or we're going to get killed, all of us here during the night.
And on that brick factory in every 100, 150 feet, it was a guard that was watching outside of the camp.
And me and my two other buddies of mine, we had no choice.
I went up to the wall and I jumped the German.
And the other two friend of mine jumped over me and we hit him until he was unconscious.
I took his clothing and his gun.
I dressed up as a German officer.
Supposedly, it was an officer at the time.
Well, we know that it was rank, you know, on his shoulder.
And I took the gun.
And I told the two friends of mine to go in front of me.
And that's the way, you know, we went into the woods.
And during the night, you know, we find out, you know, we don't know which way to go.
And again, what happened at that time, you know, that we you saw that the Germans starting to surrounding at that time coming back.
And we saw that we have no choice, that we have to do something.
So I went and I get back my old clothing what I was.
And we hid out to one home what was nearby today wood area there.
And we had the same problem, you know, that people were scared.
They was very nice to us, I have to admit it.
They gave us some bread to take, but they said, please.
Go away.
When they find though that you here, you know, they're going to kill us.
And we started to walk again around the camp-- around the woods-- until we find again that the people who was marching.
And we had no choice to again, you know, go back to the people who was marching on the street, you know, thus to save our life again.
Approximately 2,000.
Right.
The strongest people, the youngest people, they put one side.
And the older people and the weak people, they was putting it in another side.
And I recall that when we went into the camp, you know, it was a very cold, winter day.
And there were some benches there.
They let people, you know, that forced us to sit in the benches still.
We get into the barracks.
And even there, you know, a lot of people couldn't stand the cold.
You know, it was just falling down and like flies.
You know, it was dying there.
And by late night, during the night, you know, they put us into a camp there which we call the barracks.
You know, again, the same way-- it was four or five stories high, beds, you know, with hay or just a blanket, whatever.
That's the way-- was our headquarters to sleep.
I was there approximately, I would say, about 60 days or so while they was again looking for volunteers to go out for work.
And, as I say, you know, I was a volunteer.
But I always felt, you know, I might get into a better position or a better camp.
And from Auschwitz, they took us to Birkenau.
Well, it was fairing-- food I can imagine wasn't so good.
But it was some hot water with some little things in it.
We was-- at least we was getting something, you know, that you couldn't starve to death.
But it wasn't so good to stay, you know, all day long in a barracks.
And if I recall, you know, it was very little time, very little exercise they give us, you know, very little to do.
So most of the time, we stood in the barracks.
Well, everybody was for survival, you know.
And I was one of them.
You know, like everybody said, you know, when, you know, that I-- who survived, it was a hero.
We wasn't heroes.
We was survivors.
We was talking how we can survive, who was weak, starting to get weaker, you know, and so on.
But it was a very bad situation at that particular camp, you know.
I recall, you know, that we didn't have too much talk between us.
Everybody was scared, you know, that we was heard talking us, you know?
They took us out, you know, and somebody disappeared.
Many people walked out of the barracks and never come back.
So after about 60 days, they move you again.
They moved me, yes.
Then I went to called Birkenau, where I was working in an airplane factory.
They put us to work there.
They ask, you know, what kind of work I did.
I told them I was a mechanic.
So they put me in an airplane factory to work.
And on my head, you know, that, again, you know, was a survivor for me.
Everybody was getting a pack of cigarette a week.
And that was for working, you know, in that factory.
And for one cigarette I could buy food from mine German who was watching us.
You know, they was getting one pack of cigarettes, you know, what we was getting.
So that was another way to survive.
For food.
I remember, you know, in an airplane factory, I was welding, you know?
Putting on the sides, you know?
Like welding, which I never had it in my hand, you know, but I had no choice.
That's what I did at that time, you know, to do what I can far as working concern, you know, that kind of position.
Same thing.
You know, barracks, you know.
But it was a little better, you know.
And wintertime still we got a little warm.
You know, wasn't then as in Auschwitz, you know.
It was-- some of the windows broken and open.
And so we had a better situation in Birkenau than we had in Auschwitz.
Well, we get up early in the morning.
We was getting into-- I was working approximately, again, about a mile, mile and a half to the factory where they were making the planes.
And by seven o'clock, we were there and we start our day.
And they brought us food over there and was give us, you know, a half hour to eat and then back to the work place.
I worked about five, six o'clock at night, and back to the camp.
No, only men.
Only men again.
And how long do you estimate you were in Birkenau?
Birkenau I was again approximately about three months I would say, you know.
And, again, the situation came up, you know, that they was looking again for some volunteers, you know.
And I was again thought, you know, I get to a better position.
I start to put my hands up and gathered us, a lot of us.
And they from there took us to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sachsenhausen, if I recall, or-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in Auschwitz, Birkenau.
Non in Oranienburg, I'm sorry.
They took us in Oranienburg.
And there we was building-- how do you call that when they take off, the airplanes--
Runways.
That's what they put me to work, for runways.
And--
They put us in a-- how do you call them?
Wagons, you know, with a train.
And that the way they took us there.
It was a pretty good size camp, you know.
I don't know how many people really was there.
But it was a good size camp.
Yes, over there was another camp.
But it wasn't together.
It was divided, you know?
It was one side woman and one side man.
Pretty much the same.
It just was started to be a little harder than it was before.
While that was very hard work that we had to do on the runaways, you know.
And it was terrible, you know, that people over there too, you know, who couldn't work.
They just took them away, you know?
So we had to work very, very hard.
And how long do you figure you were at Oranienburg?
I would say about again a few months.
I didn't stay too long.
That's why I don't have any number on my arm, you know?
Well, I just had a neck, you know?
Number on my neck, you know.
Well, I didn't stay enough in one camp, you know?
I was moving from one camp to next camp.
I always thought it will become a better camp than the previous camp.
Did you ever arrive at a camp that you thought was better than the last one?
Not really.
They all of them was very bad.
From Oranienburg, I went to Sachsenhausen.
And that's where I was most the time till I'll get freed.
Sachsenhausen was a camp that they took us in a working to cleaning up bushes and around the camp.
So wasn't any hard work as was in the previous camp.
It was a little better.
But over there, we were sleeping on a floor.
It wasn't barracks.
Wasn't beds.
It was on the floor.
That was strictly on the floor.
Was hay and some little blankets on the top of them, and that's the way we were sleeping.
The food wasn't too bad compared to the other camp.
It was-- we was getting a couple of slices of bread a day and some hot water.
So the food was a little bit better than previous camps.
Well, that was my last camp that I've been freed.
You know, I was freed in 1945.
Well, I recall one particular time that a French jeep arrived in a camp.
But previous days, we already saw some movements-- the Germans starting to get out of the camp, you know, and cars moving left and right.
And we thought there's something happening.
And I must say that everybody was look at me where I was a strong guy.
The rest of them couldn't even stand up on their leg.
At least I could stand up on my leg and walk, you know.
Most of the people were so weak already they couldn't even walk, you know.
And they started to go to the bathroom, you know, on the way there, you know-- just happened to pass away or die while, you know, they couldn't stand on their leg.
That was a camp in the latest month, you know, when the Germans already can see, you know, that that's the end of it.
They really didn't bother too much with us already, you know.
The food was getting less and less, and it was the start of the bad situation in the last months of the camp.
A French jeep arrived, you know.
And that's the time we saw the Germans.
But maybe two, three hours before, we didn't see any Germans anymore in the camp.
So we knew something must happen.
And the jeep arrived.
And they told us, you know, that you guys going to be free now, you know, and the American army is just behind us, you know.
They should be here pretty soon, you know.
And everything will be fine.
So we really know how-- really felt at the time.
So that was our freedom, we call it.
You know, that people couldn't even really dance or-- we were so weak.
But in our heart, we felt, you know, that something is really happening.
And after this French jeep shows up, what happened after that?
What happened-- about two hours later, you know, trucks started to come with food and clothing and a lot of different thing.
And people started to get to the sick people, you know, and started to put them into a truck and take them away.
Probably took them in the hospital or whatever they could take care of them.

But in a matter of hours, you know, a lot of American cars and ambulances and all kind of help arrived at that time, which I might add, you know, at that time, which we find out is called the [INAUDIBLE] package.
You know, everybody was getting a little package which was marmalade, all kind of food in it.
And, you know, you can imagine how we started to eat there, which I would say 99% of us was getting diarrhea, you know, a matter of hours, you know, after eating that food, you know, without bread.
And it just was really a sickness that started to come out of us, you know.
And we find out, you know, that it's from the food, you know.
And they put us at a hospital at that time, which I might add at that time, I was just, like I said, the strongest people.
And I don't know-- probably was the doctor was giving me a big bottle of medicine to give everybody two pieces, you know?
And I started to do that, you know?
And I don't know what that was.
But I know that I have to give two piece to everybody that happened to be in that particular-- we called hospital.
That was a barrack.
You know, it was a, if I recall, is an army camp must have been before.
And they made a hospital out of it where they put us sick people over there.
That was near Sachsenhausen?
Near Sachsenhausen, yes.
Yes.
Recall the day what happened but I don't recall exactly--
--date was.
Yes.
I was there for about a week or 10 days until I got really a little bit stronger.
And they needed the space, you know, for more sick people than I was.
So they just give us food and they asked where we want to go.
So I happened to line up to go to Bergen-Belsen.
I didn't even know how did I get there.
But I happened to arrive to Bergen-Belsen, which that was already in an English zone.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but would say a week or 10 days later, I become the police chief of the camp.
They needed some stronger people who speak the language.
And I become the police chief.
And one of my friend, you know, who I met him here in United State and we was friends for many years, which we still are, he was head of the food distribution.
So Bergen-Belsen become-- it was a very big camp and it was well known, you know, how many people really perished there and how many died in that particular camp.
But that was very interesting work for me that I had a chance, you know, to some of my little way to pay back for the German that I went I started to hunt with my friends, you know.
And what we did, you know, we worked over a few of them and then we turned it over to the English authorities.
And I had about a year, you know, really fun to do what they did to me.
To be ordered, you know, to be people watching it, you know, not to get out of line.
And the Germans, you know, we would have any more over there.
But they had camp from civilian, from the English soldiers-- civilians, you know, who worked there, you know.
And that was my responsibility, to keep it in gear.
You know, not to get any riots or so on.
And also to help, you know, people, you know, what they needed help, you know, to get in the camp what they needed.
When-- you talk about--
When I went to Hungary, my mother and father stayed in Romania.
They put him into ghetto.
They was putting it together and they putting in the ghetto.
Since I recall, at that time, I was in Hungary, you know, with forged papers, I tried to go to see them.
I went from Hungary to Romania with-- I had Hungarian name, forged paper that I'm not a Jew and with a train, you know.
And I can imagine my feeling, you know, how I felt at that time.
But I wanted to see what I can do for my parents.
But they were surrounded already into the ghetto and I had no chance to get into that ghetto.
I met some people that I know.
And they said, you better disappear.
They're getting-- every Jews they're putting together and putting into the ghetto.
So the following day, I had no choice that I just went back to Hungary.
Well, I know that they took them also to the Germans camps, you know.
And my mother died in a concentration camp, you know.
And after the freeing, you know, I went back and I find my father and my brother in Romania.
Yes.
Well, Bergen-Belsen, again I had another job to do.
I took people from the camp back to Romania and Hungary.
I was an organizer.
I was working with the joints together.
And I had the possibility to take them back to their own countries, Hungary and Romania.
And we was getting a train.
And might as well make a really good statement.
What happened-- when I arrived in Czechoslovakia, they were treating us very good.
I went into the joint over there, you know.
They were giving us food right away.
They were treating us very well.
And we went into Hungary, which people was getting into their Hungarians-- you know, they get out of it.
And when I arrived in Romania, Romanian people came to me that they want to take away the train from us, you know.
Well, they need it for something else.
And I told them, no, I want to sell it to them.
So, anyway, you know, I was a young guy, tried to do what I can.
You know, so instead of selling it, they said they were giving us food and giving us clothing.
So, again, you know, what happened-- we get into a camp over there, you know, until everybody find-- some of them, they find their parents.
Some of them, they didn't find anybody.
So they give us shelter.
They give us everything.
It so happened that I happened to find my father and my brother at that time.
So how long do you figure you were in Bergen-Belsen?
I was in Bergen-Belsen approximately from 1945 until about 1947.
No, I didn't get paid for it really.
But I can tell you we was doing a little black market thing that we was getting from joint food stamps.
And we was treated pretty fair, you know, at the time in the camp, you know, for the past two years.
And we was starting to get our lives back.
We was playing soccer.
We was having-- you know, starting to have normal life there.
I happened to find a niece of mine, my mother's sister daughter, which I find in Bergen-Belsen.
And I will never forget almost nude.
And within a few minutes, you know, I find a polka dot dress.
She still has it.
Until this day, she will never forget the day that I brought her a polka dot dress.
And that's the only relative that survived that I find the first time.
Then again, like I said, I went back to Romania.
I brought my brother out to Germany, which my brother in 1946, I send him ahead of time to United State.
And I stood in Germany and I-- from 1947, I went up to Frankfurt am Main, which I-- that time was the joint, they call it.
You know, I went to work for them over there, again to help them for the DPs.
And I went if I recall about three times back and forth taking DPs back to their own countries, you know.
And I had again a lot of fun, you know, to follow up and get some German Nazis, you know, which we went-- was a few of us that we was dedicated to go after these particular people.
A may add what happened one time in Bergen-Belsen.
A couple of friend of mine which was, again, black marketing and they had some coffee and cigarette getting from camp to camp.
And on the way to Hamburg, the German, they caught him.
And they came back to the camp and told them that the German took away from them the whole carload of cigarette and coffee.
And without thinking twice, you know, I dressed up, you know, in an English uniform, which we had a certain uniform in the camp as a police chief.
And my men, we had some uniforms similar to the English.
And I dressed up as an English officer.
And I took two of my other people.
We also dressed up in an English uniform.
And we went up to Hamburg.
And we went into the police station.
We will find out where is they took the coffee and the cigarettes.
And we went in and I told them, you know, lock the door.
And it was about six German police at the time in a police station there.
And I told them that who was the guy who took away the cigarette and the coffee.
And, anyway, make it short, we started and we opened all the jail doors and we let everybody-- we didn't even know German, not German.
We let everybody out of there.
And they find out, you know, that in a back room, it was the coffee and the cigarettes.
They still had a lot of them.
They split it up between them, between themselves.
And about two hours later, we find out that the English police came, you know, and caught us.
And they told us what was doing here, you know, and we had no right to do this.
So they put us into a jail.
And at that time, you know, Montgomery was, you know, general at the English zone.
And his secretary, I happened to know her.
And I could give words what happened to us.
And within 24 hours, they sent us help, you know, when we get out of the jail and I went back to the camp.
So it was a little excitement, but I did it.
Tell us a little about your life in Frankfurt.
Well, Frankfurt was already-- I went into a Germany home which at that time still was not occupied.
A few friends of ours and we occupied a house.
And we was living a pretty normal life over there, already started even for the Germans getting back into normal life.
But it wasn't anything that any other people would do.
We was doing, like I said, a little black marketing.
And we have enough money already to live fairly good.
And that's what I--
Not-- sort of a work?
No.
No, absolutely not.
It was just working for ourselves, you know.
We tried to do what we can, you know, to make some money.
I was there until 1949.
In 1949, it was started to have, for myself, a little problem with the Germans, you know.
While we was stealing, you know, we felt that we had rights to do a lot of bad things for them.
Then it wasn't exactly that way.
Well, they started to get in a normal life, you know.
So I say, you know, this is the time for me to get back and get out of there.
